segments.s108-g33: 'ابا' is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'to refuse' or 'be reluctant' and here modifies the (implied) subject: 'they were unwilling'. The noun phrase 'لشکر گشن و گرز گران' follows Persian adjective order (noun + adjective), so 'لشکر گشن' = 'a hungry host' and 'گرز گران' = 'heavy maces' (گرز = mace, گران = heavy). The conjunction و links the two descriptive nouns 'گشن' and 'گرز گران' within the phrase.
segments.s108-g34: نکردند is the past plural negative of کردن ('they did not do/make'). آهنگ here is a noun 'intention/aim' used with کردن (آهنگ کردن = to make an intention / to set out toward). Word order is somewhat inverted (verb before its complement): 'نکردند آهنگِ مازندران' = 'they did not make an aim toward Mazandaran.'
segments.s108-g35: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). آن خانه = 'that house' (demonstrative + noun). دیو is 'demon' and stands before افسونگرست, a compound in which افسونگر (enchanter/enchanting) carries the copula -ست ('is'), so the phrase means 'is (the) demon-enchanter's' or more naturally 'is the den of a sorcerous demon.'
segments.s108-g36: طلسمست is طلسم + است (copula) meaning 'is talismanic/enchanted.' The conjunction و links the next clause. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of.' بند here means 'fetter/bond' (not stanza), and جادو is 'magic.' درست in the line functions as a past/adjectival form meaning 'made' or 'constructed' (poetic use); the clause says it is talismanic and 'made from the bonds of magic.'
segments.s108-g37: مران is a proper name (the monster/thing in question) and را is the definite object/topic marker attached to it. به شمشیر marks the instrument ('by/with the sword'); نتوان is the negative potential 'cannot/was not able,' and شکست is the verbal root 'to break/defeat' used after نتوان. Together: 'Maran را به شمشیر نتوان شکست' = 'Maran cannot be defeated by the sword.'
segments.s108-g38: This clause lists means that also fail: 'به گنج و به دانش' ('by treasure and by knowledge/learning'). نیاید is a negative present-form 'does not come' used impersonally with به دست ('to the hand' = into one's possession/power). The idiom means 'cannot be had/acquired by treasure or learning.'
segments.s108-g39: هم = 'also/likewise'; آن را = 'that (thing) (object marker)'. به نیرنگ is an instrumental phrase 'by trickery/deception.' نتوان is again the negative potential 'cannot,' and گشاد is the past/adjectival form meaning 'opened' (here used with نتوان to mean 'cannot be opened'). The structure is parallel to earlier clauses denying an approach.
segments.s108-g40: مده is an archaic negative imperative 'do not give/do not put out' (i.e., 'do not squander'). The list 'رنج و گنج و درم' names what must not be wasted ('toil/hardship, treasure, and dirhams/coins'). را is the object marker; به باد ('to the wind') is an idiom meaning 'in vain' or 'to waste' (باد here is the literal 'wind' used idiomatically).
segments.s108-g25: ز تو پیشتر پادشه بوده‌اند is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrasing meaning 'there were kings before you.' ز + تو = 'before you' (poetic contraction of از تو/از پیشِ تو). پادشه (subject) + بوده‌اند is a compound past/perfect ('have been' / 'were') in third person plural, so the clause says that such kings existed earlier than you.
segments.s108-g26: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that.' این راه = 'this way/road.' هرگز + نـ (negative prefix) + پیموده‌اند (perfect of پیمودن) yields 'have never traversed'; word order places the negative adverb هرگز before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s108-g27: که begins a new explanatory clause linked to the previous thought. بر سر مرا is a poetic way to say 'over my head / upon me' (مرا = me, with را as the object marker), and روز چندی گذشت is a simple past: روز چندی 'a few days' and گذشت 'passed' (lit. 'days passed upon me').
segments.s108-g28: سپهر از بر خاک چندی بگشت is pictorial: سپهر 'the sky/heavens' + از بر خاک 'away from the earth' (از بر = away from/off of) and چندی بگشت 'turned/was turned for a while.' بگشت is a past verb meaning 'turned/returned' (poetic past). The clause says the sky withdrew or turned away from the earth for a time.
segments.s108-g29: منوچهر شد زین جهان فراخ: منوچهر (subject) + شد often signals a change of state and in poetic usage can mean 'departed' or 'passed away.' زین is a poetic contraction of از این ('from this'), so the phrase means 'Manuchehr left/was gone from this wide world.' فراخ qualifies جهان as 'spacious/wide.'
segments.s108-g30: ازو ماند ایدر بسی گنج و کاخ: ازو = از او 'from him,' ماند is past 'remained/was left,' و ایدر 'here' (ایدر), and بسی (poetic adverb) = 'much/a great deal.' The phrase states that many treasures and palaces remained here from him.
segments.s108-g31: همان زو و با نوذر و کیقباد lists sources/people: همان 'that same' or 'likewise,' ز (here part of زو = از او) = 'from him,' با = 'with' or 'and' used to link the following names نوذر and کیقباد. The phrase enumerates 'from him and from Nowzar and Kay Qobad.'
segments.s108-g32: چه مایه بزرگان که داریم یاد is an exclamatory construction: چه مایه = 'what a wealth/source (of...)', بزرگان 'the great ones' (heroes/forebears), که introduces the clause 'that we have,' and داریم یاد = 'we hold in memory / we remember.' Together: 'what a store of great men we remember.'
segments.s108-g17: چنین ('thus') is an adverb introducing reported speech. گفت is the speech verb 'said/told' (here used with the enclitic مر 'to me'), so the phrase means 'thus (he) told me'. The following words 'شاه را زال زر' name the speaker in inverted order: زال زر (Zal the Golden) is the person who is speaking, and شاه را functions as part of the reported clause to follow; the word‑order is typical poetic inversion.
segments.s108-g18: که introduces the reported content ('that ...'). نوشه is a noun 'draught/cup', modified by بدی 'evil, ill‑omened' (an attributive adjective), so نوشه بدی = 'an ill‑omened draught'. شاه و پیروزگر are the subject (the king and the victorious one) of the embedded clause who 'drank' that draught; the verb of drinking is implied by the poetic context rather than explicitly repeated here.
segments.s108-g19: همه is the universal subject 'all/everyone'. شاد و روشن are predicative adjectives 'happy and bright/enlivened'. به بخت ('by fortune/fate') indicates cause or condition. تواند is the classical third‑person singular optative/ability verb 'may/shall be able' used here with a collective subject — a common poetic agreement pattern.
segments.s108-g20: برافراخته ('raised/upright') modifies سر ('head') to mean 'head held high'. به تخت ('onto the throne') gives the goal/direction. توانَد repeats the modal/ability verb; the clause as a whole expresses that they will be able to lift their heads onto the throne — another classical optative/paraphrased future.
segments.s108-g21: ازان پس ('after that/thereupon') is a temporal phrase starting the narrative. یکی داستان کرد literally 'one (someone) did/made a story' — idiomatically 'someone told a story'. یاد here functions as 'remembrance/recalling' and links to the next segment (the thing being recalled). کرد is the simple past of 'to do/make' used idiomatically with داستان to mean 'recounted'.
segments.s108-g22: سخنهای شایسته را is the object phrase 'the worthy/fitting words' (سخنهای = plural of speech/words, را marking it as definite object). در گشاد ('in the wide/spacious') is an adverbial phrase describing the place — in epic Persian 'گشاد' commonly names the open hall or wide assembly‑space where speech is recounted.
segments.s108-g23: چنین گفت introduces another quoted utterance. کای is the vocative particle 'O (Kay)'; here it addresses the royal addressee. پادشاه جهان 'king of the world' is an honorific title in apposition; the whole phrase functions as direct address at the start of the quoted speech.
segments.s108-g24: سزاوار is a predicative adjective 'deserving/fit for'. تختی and تاج are the objects 'a throne and a crown' (تختی is a nominalized/partitive form of تخت). مهان follows تاج as a modifier meaning 'of the great/noble (men)' — together 'the crown of the great' — completing the praise of the addressee as fit for royal insignia.
segments.s108-g49: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('whose' / 'that'), linking this description to something mentioned earlier. بار و بلندیش pairs two nouns: بار (load/weight/burden) and بلندیش (بلندی + -ش, 'its height/loftiness' — the possessive enclitic -ش ’its’), joined by و. نفرین بود is the predication in past tense: نفرین (curse) + بود (was). Word order is typical older/poetic Persian (subject/possessed phrases before the copula).
segments.s108-g50: نه negates the following clause ('not'). آیین شاهان پیشین is a noun phrase: آیین (custom/practice) modified by شاهان پیشین ('the kings of old' — شاهان = kings, پیشین = former/ancient). بود is the past copula 'was'. Together: 'nor was it the custom/practice of former kings.'
segments.s108-g51: چنین modifies the reported speech ('thus' / 'so') and پاسخ is the object 'answer'. آورد is the past verb 'brought/uttered' (here 'gave/said'), with کاووس as the subject; باز 'again' is an adverb placed after the name for emphasis. The sequence is typical of poetic word order: adverb/object + verb + subject + adverb.
segments.s108-g52: کز introduces the content of what was said ('that' / 'for'), followed by اندیشه تو 'your thought/counsel' (اندیشه + possessive تو). نیم here modifies بی‌نیاز and means 'half/partly' (not a full negation), so نیم بی‌نیاز = 'not wholly free / only half free'. The clause thus says he was not entirely free of concern/affected by your thought. Word order places the modifier نیم before the predicate adjective بی‌نیاز.
segments.s108-g53: ولیکن ('but/yet') marks contrast. من is the nominative 'I'. از introduces the comparison/source ('from' / 'than' in comparative sense here) followed by the names آفریدون و جم (Afaridun and Jam) — i.e. 'I, compared with Afaridun and Jam'.
segments.s108-g54: فزونم is a predicate meaning 'I am more/exceed' with the first‑person suffix -م ('I am greater'). به introduces the areas of excess ('in/regarding'): مردی (manliness/manhood), و فر (and far / 'royal glory' — فر), و درم (and derham/coin — wealth). The list after به specifies the respects in which the speaker claims superiority.
segments.s108-g55: همان here means 'the same' or 'likewise' — it picks up the previous assertion and applies it to the following kings. از introduces each name ('from/of Manuchehr and of Kay Qobad'), so the phrase enumerates those other kings to whom the same applies.
segments.s108-g56: که is the relative/connector 'who/that' linking to the named kings. مازندران را uses را as the definite object marker ('Mazandaran' as the object/topic). نکردند is the past negative plural 'they did not do' (here 'they did not make/remember'), and یاد ('remembrance') is the noun completing the verbal idea; the poetic order places نکردند before یاد: 'they did not make remembrance' = 'they did not remember Mazandaran.'
segments.s108-g73: This clause expresses a conditional: the speaker addresses the addressee as a potential companion in battle. گرایدونک functions as an introductory/intonation particle here (poetic), یارم is the possessive/vocative 'my friend/companion', and نباشی is a present/subjunctive/negative form meaning 'you are not / you will not be' (second person). The prepositional phrase به جنگ ('into/for battle') follows the verb phrase, matching Persian SOV tendencies in poetry.
segments.s108-g74: Imperative/request structure: مفرمای is an honorific imperative 'command / please do (say)', followed by the object pronoun phrase ما را ('us' with the definite/object marker). بدین is here formed with the prefix ب- plus دین in an older poetic form meaning roughly 'give to us / grant us' (treated as part of the verb phrase), and در درنگ is a locative/temporal phrase 'in/at delay' i.e. 'without delay' or 'do not tarry here' — درنگ (delay) is the noun and the preceding در is the locative sense 'in/at'.
segments.s108-g75: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از شاه marks the source/agent 'from the king'; بنشنید here functions as a past verb 'heard' (poetic past), and the patient/topic of that hearing is این سخن 'this speech'. Word order follows classical Persian: temporal/subordinate conjunction + agent phrase + verb + topic.
segments.s108-g76: This clause is negative-past observation: ندید is the negative past of 'see' ('he did not see'), followed by ایچ پیدا 'anything/apparent' (ایچ = 'nothing/none' and پیدا = 'apparent/visible'). سرش را is the possessed direct object 'his head', and ز + بن is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the root/base' (ز = 'from', بن = 'root, base'). Together it means he perceived nothing visible of his head from its root.
segments.s108-g77: بدو is a prepositional/dative phrase 'to him' (to that person), گفت here is the speech verb 'said' (used as the reportive verb), and شاهی indicates the speaker's identity/role 'the king' or 'royal [one]'. The coordinating و links to the following self-reference ما بنده‌ایم 'we are servants' (present copular statement).
segments.s108-g78: این is omitted here; the phrase begins with به دلسوزگی 'out of compassion' where به = adverbial preposition 'by/with' and دلسوزگی = 'compassion / pity'. با تو 'with you' marks the addressee, and گوینده‌ایم is a first-person plural present-form 'we are speaking' or 'we speak' (a humble/formal plural used by the speaker). The whole segment gives the motive and addressee for the speech.
segments.s108-g79: This protasis begins with اگر 'if'. داد here is the noun 'justice' (or the notion of just action/justice) modifying فرمان ('command'), so the phrase reads 'if (you) give just command' or 'if justice/just command is given'. دهی‌گر is a compound/poetic form attached to the conditional sense (in older Persian it can function to continue the conditional idea); ستم following marks the counterfactual/contrast 'oppression / injustice' (i.e., if despite justice oppression occurs). The sequence sets up a conditional contrast.
segments.s108-g80: برای تو is a dative phrase 'for you' or 'on your behalf'; باید is the modal 'must / it is necessary'; the infinitival/gerundial زدن + گام is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to take/strike a step' (i.e., to act), and و دم adds a short object 'and breath/moment' (in poetry meaning 'immediately' or 'in an instant'). Together the clause prescribes immediate action: you must act at once.
segments.s108-g65: چنان is an adverb of degree meaning “so/thus” placed before the adjectives to intensify them; زار و خوارند is a compound predication where زار and خوار (adjectives) are joined by و and the copular -ند marks plural/state (“they are wretched and humiliated”). بر چشم من is a locative phrase literally “on my eye,” i.e. “in my sight”; Persian order here is verb/adjective phrase before the locative adjunct.
segments.s108-g66: The paired چه ... چه is an exclamative/correlative construction meaning roughly “what ... and what ... / whether ... or ...” used to emphasize the two offending elements; جادو and دیوان are the two nouns being contrasted. آن انجمن is a determiner + noun (“that assembly”), with آن pointing to the previously mentioned gathering.
segments.s108-g67: به گوش تو آید is a common classical Persian phrasing where the prepositional phrase ‘to your ear’ precedes the verb آید (“comes”) and means “you will hear.” خود این آگهی places خود (“self/very”) before این آگهی to mean “this very news” (emphatic). Word order is Topic (to your ear) + Verb + Emphatic NP.
segments.s108-g68: کزیشان is a fused archaic relative/causal expression (کز + ایشان) meaning “from them / because of them”; شود is the classical present/future auxiliary ‘become/will be,’ here indicating a resultant state; روی گیتی تهی literally ‘the face/surface of the world empty’ — adjective (تهی) after the noun phrase, giving “the world’s face will be empty.”
segments.s108-g69: This is an imperative construction: تو (you) as subject is followed by the prepositional accompaniment با رستم (“with Rostam”), then the locative/scene marker ایدر (“here”), and the nominal predicate جهاندار with the imperative باش (“be”). In Persian imperatives the copula ‘be’ (باش) comes at the end.
segments.s108-g70: نگهبان ایران is a noun phrase ‘guardian/protector of Iran’ serving as the complement of the implied imperative ‘be’; the coordinating و links the next adjective بیدار, and the repeated باش is the second imperative ‘be (awake).’ The sequence gives two commands: be guardian and be alert.
segments.s108-g71: جهان آفریننده is a genitive/attributive phrase ‘the world's Creator’ or ‘the Creator of the world’; یار منست is a poetic copular expression where منست functions like ‘is mine / is my ally’ (an older/poetic form of ‘است’ with a possessive nuance). Word order: possessor/descriptor + predicate + poetic copula.
segments.s108-g72: سر here means ‘chief/leader’ and is followed by نره (male) modifying دیوان (here contextually ‘divs/demons’ in the epic idiom); شکار منست repeats the poetic copula construction to mean ‘is my prey/quarry.’ The noun شکار after the subject phrase denotes what is taken or hunted; the whole phrase states possession/result (‘is my quarry’).
segments.s108-g97: This is a word-order typical of classical Persian: the indirect object marked by به (be) comes first (به زال = 'to Zāl'), then the temporal adverb آنگهی ('then'), followed by the verb گفت ('said') and the subject گیو (Giv). از خدای is a prepositional phrase 'from God' serving as the object of the following verb phrase.
segments.s108-g98: همی here is the archaic/prosodic prefix that marks an ongoing or prospective action before a present/future verb (hamī‑). خواهم is first-person present/future 'I will / I intend'. آنک is a conjunction 'that' introducing the subordinate clause; او بود رهنمای = 'he be [a] guide' uses بود (classical past/infinitive of 'to be') in an optative/subjunctive-like sense ('that he be / may he be') relative to the request.
segments.s108-g99: به جایی 'to a place' sets the location; که here functions as a relative/locative conjunction 'where/that'; کاووس را uses the object/topic marker را but here marks Kavus as the one possessing access; دسترس is the noun 'reach/access' and completes the locative phrase 'a place where Kavus has no reach.'
segments.s108-g100: نباشد is the negative copula/verb 'is not / there is not' (here linked back to 'دسترس' = 'has no access'). ندارم is first-person present 'I do not have / I will not [give/allow]'; مر is an archaic clitic meaning roughly 'to/for me' or marking the speaker's interest; او را is the definite object marker + 'him'; به کس = 'to anyone'. Together they express 'I will not give/entrust him to anyone.'
segments.s108-g101: This clause is an optative/blessing introduced elliptically by باد after دور: ز تو دور باد literally 'may ... be far from you.' آز و چشم نیاز are the two things wished away: 'greed and the pleading/supplicating eye' (a poetic collocation meaning greed and begging).
segments.s108-g102: مبادا is a negative optative/precaution 'may it not be / lest'; به تو 'toward you'; دست دشمن دراز literally 'the enemy's hand long' — in idiomatic Persian دراز here means 'extended' or 'reach out', so the clause means 'may the enemy's hand not be extended toward you.'
segments.s108-g103: به هر سو 'to every side/wherever' + که آییم 'that we come/wherever we go' and اندر شویم 'and enter/go in' — a correlative pair meaning 'in every direction that we come and enter.' The verbs are first-person plural (آییم, شویم) in classical/formal subjunctive/indicative forms used for generality.
segments.s108-g104: جز او 'except him' restricts the following noun phrase; آفرینت سخن is a poetic way to say 'words of praise/acclamation' (آفرینت = acclamation/praise), and نشنویم is first‑person plural negative 'we do not hear / may we not hear' — together: 'may we hear no praise except his.'
segments.s108-g9: چو here introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when' / 'as'); the phrase تختِ تو (throne + 2nd-person pronoun) is the grammatical subject. نشنید and ندید are simple past 3rd-person singular verbs applied poetically to inanimate things (personification); و simply coordinates the two predicates ('did not hear and did not see').
segments.s108-g10: نه چون begins a contrastive clause ('nor/as not'); بخت تو is 'your fate' and چرخ گردان is a compound where گردان ('revolving') modifies چرخ ('wheel' here, i.e. the wheel of fortune). شنید is another past 3rd-person verb; the whole clause continues the negation/contrast with the previous clause.
segments.s108-g11: همه‌ساله is an adverbial ('every year'); the adjectives پیروز and شاد are predicated of the addressee. بادی here is an archaic/poetic optative/benedictive form ('may you be') that yields the wish construction 'همه‌ساله پیروز بادی و شاد' = 'may you be victorious and happy every year.'
segments.s108-g12: This is a parallel nominal clause describing wished qualities. سرت ('your head') + پر ز + دانش = 'your head full of knowledge'; دلت is دل + enclitic ت (2nd-person possessive). The adjective-like construction 'پر ز X' means 'full of X' and continues the optative sense established earlier.
segments.s108-g13: شه نامبردار is an NP ('the famed king' or 'king, famed in name') functioning as the name/title being given. بنواختش is an archaic poetic verb form with the enclitic object ـش (him); here it means an action of bestowing/naming/announcing (the exact lexical nuance is poetic), with ـش marking the person affected.
segments.s108-g14: بر خویش (خوی + enclitic ش = 'his own') and بر تخت ('upon the throne') give the place where the person was set. بنشاختش is a past verb with the enclitic ـش repeating the object ('he set/placed him upon his own throne'); بر marks the locative 'on/upon.'
segments.s108-g15: بپرسیدش is an imperative/command form meaning 'ask him' (the verbal prefix بـ plus پرسید in a jussive/imperative sense) with enclitic ـش 'him' as object. از introduces the topic ('about/concerning'), and رنج راه دراز literally orders as 'the hardship of the long road' (رنج modifying the phrase راه دراز).
segments.s108-g16: ز گردان 'concerning the troop' continues the list introduced by از; و links to another topic introduced by از: 'and about Rostam, sarfraz.' سرفراز is an epithet modifying رستم ('Rostam the victorious').
segments.s108-g57: This is a nominal list with an adjectival/copular predicate at the end. سپاه (army), دل (heart; here = courage/inner strength) and گنجم (گنج + ـم, a possessive suffix 'my' = 'my treasure') are coordinated by و. افزونترست is an older/poetic fused form of افزون‌تر است (comparative + copula) meaning “is more/are greater,” so the whole segment states that these three things are greater/more abundant.
segments.s108-g58: This is a short clause with subject جهان (the world) followed by a prepositional phrase. زیر here functions as an instrumental/positional preposition 'under / subject to', شمشیر تیز is a noun + adjective ('sharp sword'), and اندرست is an archaic/fused form of اندر است = 'is within/under'; together: 'the world is under the sharp sword.'
segments.s108-g59: چو is the conjunction 'when'. بردانشی is a poetic/old noun (here a calamity/sickness/affliction — the line uses a harsh image) and شد is the past-tense verb 'became'; گشاده (open, laid bare) modifies جهان (world). Word order: temporal clause (چو + noun) + شد + predicate adjective + subject (جهان) produces 'when the world became laid open by the affliction.'
segments.s108-g60: This is a rhetorical question. به آهن uses به with an instrumental/agentive sense 'by/with iron' (iron/steel as instrument = sword), چه داریم is the interrogative 'what do we have' (چه preceding the verb), and گیتی نهان is a postpositive phrase 'hidden thing(s) of the world' (گیتی = the world; نهان = hidden). Literal order: 'by iron what do we have, (that is) the world's hidden [thing]?'
segments.s108-g61: شوم‌شان combines شوم (subjunctive/optative of شدن 'to become' / here a volitive formula often meaning 'may I be —' or 'let me be the cause of…') with the clitic شان 'them/their', giving a purposive/benedictory flavor directed at 'them'. یکایک is an adverb 'one by one', به راه آوردم is a verbal phrase 'bring/put onto the road' (آوردن governs the object; verb آوردم appears in final position as first-person singular). Overall: 'I will bring them one by one onto the road.'
segments.s108-g62: گر is conditional 'if'. آیین here means 'custom/practice' (not a liturgical ritual necessarily). شمشیر و گاه are coordinated objects: 'the custom/way of the sword and (of) the moment/time' (گاه = the opportune time), and آورم (I bring/introduce) is repeated as the main verb for the conditional clause; syntactically it gives the condition under which the preceding action will be done: 'if I introduce the custom of the sword and of the occasion.'
segments.s108-g63: اگر is the conditional 'if'. کس here functions in an indefinite/negative sense 'anyone / no one' (context makes it 'no one'), and نمانم is first-person singular ('I leave/remain'); together in this idiom it means 'if I leave no one in Mazandaran' (بهِ مازندران marks the location 'in / to Mazandaran'). The verb is 1st person because the speaker is stating a possible action he himself might do.
segments.s108-g64: وگر = 'or if / but if'. بر نهم is the verb نَهَم (I put/place) with the preposition بر 'upon/on' (here idiomatic: 'I unleash / set upon'). باژ و ساو are two coordinated items (باژ can mean 'cry/noise'; ساو is here likely a proper name/place), and گران is an adjective 'heavy/mighty' that qualifies them (poetic ordering places the adjective after the nouns). The clause thus says 'or if I set upon them the mighty Baž and Sāv.'
segments.s108-g1: همی رفت is a poetic past form where همی gives a continuous/iterative sense to the past verb رفت ('he went/kept going'). پیش here functions as a directional preposition 'toward/in front of' and is followed by اندرون زال 'Zal's inner quarters' (in poetry genitive relation is shown by juxtaposition). زر is a bare noun 'gold' attached after the place-phrase to indicate he carried or was accompanied by gold (an instrumental/adjunct noun rather than a separate clause).
segments.s108-g2: پس here means 'after/behind' (spatial/temporal 'behind him'); او is the pronoun 'he' and بزرگان is a plural noun 'the great ones/elders' used as the subject/phrase. زرین کمر is a compound adjective+noun 'golden‑waisted' or 'girded with gold' describing those great men. The clause omits an explicit copula or verb (a common poetic ellipsis); the meaning is 'behind him were the great men with golden belts.'
segments.s108-g3: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کاووس را دید is a clause '(he) saw Kay Kavus' with را marking the definite object. The following phrase دستان سام functions as an epithet/appositive—literally 'Sam of the hands' (i.e. 'the strong‑handed Sam')—so the whole clause reads 'when (the) strong‑handed Sam saw Kay Kavus.'
segments.s108-g4: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'seated' (here used as a finite-state description), بر اورنگ is the prepositional phrase 'upon the throne' (بر + اورنگ), and بر شادکام is another prepositional phrase 'in/with joy' (شادکام = 'joyful/fortunate'). Together they describe the subject (Sam) as 'seated on the throne, joyful.'
segments.s108-g5: به کش کرده دست is a participial/instrumental phrase: به + کش + کرده (here 'کش' from 'kesh' = 'stretch/pull' with کرده as participle) + دست — literally 'with hand stretched out' or 'having stretched out his hand'. و connects the next descriptors; سرافگنده پست are two adjectives 'ashamed/humiliated' and 'low/abased' that jointly modify the (unspecified) subject. The whole phrase functions adverbially to describe the manner of his approach.
segments.s108-g6: همی رفت is again the continuous past 'he went on/kept going'. تا introduces the limit 'until' and takes جایگاه 'the place/seat' as its object; نشست is the perfect/past 'sat' (here a main verb), so the clause means 'he went on until he reached the place and sat down.' The sequence همی رفت تا ... نشست expresses motion continuing up to the point of sitting.
segments.s108-g7: چنین گفت is the formulaic 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here functioning as 'said' in narrative formulae). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and کدخدای جهان is the addressee phrase 'kadhkhoda of the world' (a lofty title formed by compound noun + جهان 'world'). The whole segment introduces direct address.
segments.s108-g8: سرافراز is an epithet 'honored/exalted'. بر is the preposition 'over/above' and governs the plural nouns مهتران و مهان ('the elders/leading men' and 'the guests/visitors'); the construction means 'exalted above the elders and the guests.' This is a short vocative/complimentary clause following the opening 'O ...'.
segments.s108-g105: پس از is a compound prepositional expression meaning “after” (پس + از). کردگار جهان‌آفرین is a noun phrase: کردگار (the Creator/God) modified by the epithet جهان‌آفرین (“world‑creator”); the epithet follows the noun in this poetic register.
segments.s108-g106: This is an inverted Persian clause: به تو (dative 'to/for you') marks the target of the hope, دارد is the present/simple verb 'has/possesses', امید is the noun 'hope', and ایران‌زمین (the land of Iran) is the (topical) subject placed at the end. Literally: 'to you has hope Iran‑land' → 'the land of Iran places its hope in you.'
segments.s108-g107: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional cluster meaning 'for the sake of' / 'because of'; گوان is an archaic demonstrative/particle meaning 'such' / 'those (ways/reasons)' qualifying the cause. رنج is the object 'hardship/suffering', and برداشتی is a past tense verb (second person singular) 'you took up / you bore' — i.e. 'you endured'.
segments.s108-g108: چنین 'thus' introduces the result; راه دشوار is the noun phrase 'a difficult path' (راه = way, دشوار = difficult). بگذاشتی is the past tense (2nd person singular) of گذاشتن with the archaic prefix ب‑, meaning 'you set/you placed/you made' — here 'you set (a) difficult course/road'.
segments.s108-g109: پس آنگه is a doubled temporal adverb 'then / afterwards' for emphasis. گرفتندش is the past plural verb 'they took/seized him' with the enclitic object ‑ش 'him/it'. اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'to/at the side; aside', indicating where they led or placed him.
segments.s108-g110: ره سیستان را: ره 'way/road' qualified by سیستان 'Sistan', with را marking the definite object. برآراست is a past tense verb 'arranged / put in order' (third person form used here in a narrative sequence), and کار 'work/affair' names the undertaking; classical/poetic word order places object before verb and the substantive (کار) after the verb, so the clause means 'they arranged/put in order the work (i.e. set about the task) for the road to Sistan.'
segments.s108-g81: از اندیشه is an ablative/prepositional phrase ('from/out of thought'). دل را is the definite/direct object (دل + object marker را) placed before the verb, following Persian SOV tendencies in poetry. بپرداختم is 1st‑person singular past of پرداختن in a literary sense; here it governs دل را and indicates turning/doing something to the heart (contextually 'removing' or 'setting free' it from thought).
segments.s108-g82: سخن is the noun 'speech/words' and functions as the thing acted on by the verb. آنچ (آن‌چِ = آن‌چه) is a relative/compound pronoun 'that which/what'. دانستم is 1st‑person past of 'دانستن' used here in the sense 'I thought/deemed'. انداختم is 1st‑person past of انداختن ('to throw/place'); combined the clause means 'I cast forth/uttered the words that I judged (so)'.
segments.s108-g83: نه fronting the clause negates it. مرگ is the noun 'death', followed by the ablative phrase از تن خویش ('out of/from one's own body'); خوی + ش together function as خویش 'one's own' (ش is the possessive clitic). بتون/بتوان here is the modal 'can/be able' (classical/poetic form) and سپوخت is a past form of 'to burn/consume' — together indicating inability: 'death cannot be burned away from the body.'
segments.s108-g84: نه negates the clause. چشم جهان is a genitive phrase 'the eye of the world' (چشم + جهان). کس is the indefinite subject 'anyone/someone'. به سوزن is an instrumental phrase 'with a needle', placed before the verb for emphasis. بدوخت is past of دوختن ('to sew'), used metaphorically here: 'no one sewed/stiched the world's eye with a needle.'
segments.s108-g85: به پرهیز is a prepositional phrase 'to/for abstinence' (praise of restraint). هم is an emphasizer 'even/also'. کس is the indefinite subject 'no one/anyone' (here negative sense carried by the context). نجست is past of جستن used here in the sense 'sought/attempted'. از نیاز is a complement 'from/away from desire/need' — overall: 'no one even sought abstinence from desire.'
segments.s108-g86: جهانجوی is a compound noun 'world‑seeker' (an ambitious person) and is the grammatical subject. ازین سه ('from these three') is an ablative/partitive phrase (از + این with the poetic spelling ین). نیابد is a present/past negative form of یافتن used here in the sense 'will not find/attain'. جواز is the object 'permission/justification' — 'a world‑seeker will not obtain justification from these three.'
segments.s108-g87: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. جهان is the grammatical subject 'the world' (or 'this life'). بر تو is the prepositional beneficiary 'toward you'. فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate'. باد here is the optative particle (classical Persian 'may it be'), so the clause is a wish: 'May the world always be auspicious to you.'
segments.s108-g88: مبادا is a negative optative/concessive ('may it not be', 'lest'). که introduces the content clause. پند من is 'my counsel/advice' (possession signified by the order). آیدت is a compound verb 'comes to you' (آید + clitic ت for 'you'), and یاد is 'remembrance/memory' — the collocation آیدت یاد = 'may my counsel come to your remembrance' (here wished not to happen): 'Lest my counsel come to your mind.'
segments.s108-g41: همایون here is a noun meaning 'auspicious fortune' (not the personal name). ندارد is the present negated auxiliary 'does not have'. The phrase کس آنجا شدن is an infinitival/verbal cluster ('someone to become/be there') placed after the negated verb; in effect the line says 'no one there possesses auspiciousness for becoming' — classical Persian allows this inverted order and omits an explicit copula.
segments.s108-g42: وزایدر is an archaic plural/compound referring to the ministers/counsellors (subject). کنون is 'now'. رای is a noun 'counsel/decision', and the following two verbs رفتن زدن are infinitives/masdars expressing the content of that counsel: 'to go' and 'to strike/attack'. The construction means 'the ministers' present counsel is (to) go and strike.'
segments.s108-g43: سپه را uses the object marker را to make 'the army' definite/emphatic. بران سو is an archaic compound meaning 'toward that side' (بر + آن + سو). نباید is the impersonal prohibition 'one must not / should not', followed by کشید, the infinitive/preterite-stem of کشیدن used here to mean 'to draw/lead/march' — i.e. 'the army must not be led to that side.'
segments.s108-g44: ز شاهان کس is an inverted expression meaning 'of the kings, no one' (i.e. 'no king'). این رای 'this counsel/plan' is the object, هرگز ندید is the past 'has ever seen' turned into 'has ever adopted/known' (negative universal with هرگز). Together: 'no king has ever seen/adopted this plan.'
segments.s108-g45: This is a comparative clause: (گرین) نامداران ترا کهترند = 'those/these famed men are lesser/inferior than you.' گرین is an archaic/uncertain demonstrative (not common in modern Persian) and is treated here as a demonstrative modifier; نامداران is the plural 'renowned ones'; ترا is the object of comparison ('to you'); کهترند is the plural predicate 'are inferior.'
segments.s108-g46: چنین 'such/so' modifies بنده 'servant/humble one' (here plural/collective sense 'those humble ones'). دادگر is an adjective/noun 'just, upright', and داورند is the present plural copular/predicative form 'they are judges' or 'they judge.' Poetic inversion places adjectives and predicates before the verb; the phrase asserts that such (humble) people are just judges.
segments.s108-g47: تو از خون چندین سرنامدار is a genitive/prepositional phrase 'you are of the blood (lineage) of several illustrious (ancestors)'. The copula 'are' is omitted (as usual in Persian poetry). چندین is a distributive/indefinite numeral 'several' modifying سرنامدار 'illustrious (ones)'.
segments.s108-g48: ز بهر is an archaic compound preposition meaning 'for the sake of' (ز + بهر). فزونی 'increase/superiority' is its object: 'for the sake of greater power/advantage.' درختی is a nominative with the -ی suffix meaning 'a tree' used metaphorically, and مکار is an adjective 'deceitful/treacherous.' The phrase predicates back on 'تو' (you): 'you, for the sake of gain, are a treacherous tree.'
segments.s108-g89: This clause is an optative wish built around the adjective 'پشیمان' (regretful). The sequence 'پشیمان مبادی ز کردار خویش' should be read as an old/poetic optative: 'پشیمان مباد(ی) ز کردار خویش' = 'may [you/he] not be regretful because of his own deeds'. 'ز' is the preposition 'from/because of' linking 'پشیمان' to the cause 'کردار خویش'. The final element is a possessive clitic attached to 'خوی' (rendered here as separate tokens 'خوی' + 'ش').
segments.s108-g90: 'به تو باد' is a conventional optative construction meaning 'may it be to you / may you have'. Here 'باد' is the optative particle 'may (it be)'. What follows are the qualities wished for: 'روشن‌دل' (clear‑hearted) and 'دین و کیش' (religion and creed), joined by 'و' as simple coordination.
segments.s108-g91: Word order here is adverb + object + subject + verb: 'سبک' (lightly) modifies the verb phrase 'زال پدرود کرد' (Zāl bade farewell). 'شاه را' is marked by the direct‑object marker 'را' (the king being the addressee/target of the farewell). 'پدرود کرد' is a light periphrastic construction where the noun 'پدرود' (farewell) is turned into an action by the verb 'کرد'.
segments.s108-g92: The clause 'دل از رفتن او پر از دود کرد' states a result: 'دل ... پر از دود کرد' = 'made (his) heart full of smoke'. 'از رفتن او' is a causal/ablative phrase ('from/because of his leaving'). 'پر' is an adjective 'full (of)' governed by the following 'از' which here means 'of' before the noun 'دود'. The verb 'کرد' again turns the description into a causative/result 'made [it] so'.
segments.s108-g93: 'برون آمد' is the compound verb meaning 'went out' or 'came forth'. It is followed by the prepositional phrase 'از پیش کاووس شاه' = 'from before King Kāvus', where 'پیش' in this context means 'in the presence of / before'. Word order (verb then source phrase) is normal in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s108-g94: This clause has the perfective passive/experiential 'شده' + adjective 'تیره' = 'has become dark'. 'بر چشم او' (= 'on/in his eye') expresses the locus of perception, so the whole means '... became dark in his eyes'. The real grammatical subject ('هور و ماه' = 'sun and moon' or metaphorical 'splendor and moon') appears after the predicate, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s108-g95: 'برفتند' is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they departed/went'. 'با او' marks accompaniment 'with him'. 'بزرگان' is the plural subject 'the great men/nobles'. The token 'نیو' is uncertain here (textual/orthographic variant); it does not fit a clear meaning from the provided senses and is probably an archaic/poetic or corrupt form — the clause functions to say the nobles accompanied him.
segments.s108-g96: This is a simple enumeration introduced by 'چو' used as 'such as'/'like' to list prominent names who accompanied him: 'چو طوس و چو گودرز و رهام و گیو' = 'such as Tus and Goudarz and Roham and Giv'. 'چو' functions as a marker introducing examples rather than a temporal 'when'.

sentences.s108-s17: They, reluctant, did not set their aim on Mazandaran with a hungry host and heavy maces.
sentences.s108-s18: For that house is the den of a sorcerous demon; it is talismanic and made from magic's bonds.
sentences.s108-s19: Maran cannot be overcome by the sword; it cannot be obtained by treasure or by learning.
sentences.s108-s20: Nor can it be opened by trickery; do not waste toil, treasure, and coin to the wind.
sentences.s108-s13: There have been kings before you who have never traveled this road.
sentences.s108-s14: For several days there passed above my head; the sky for a while turned away from the earth.
sentences.s108-s15: Manuchehr departed from this wide world; from him there remained here many treasures and palaces.
sentences.s108-s16: From him, and likewise from Nowzar and Kay Qobad, what a store of great men we remember!
sentences.s108-s9: Thus Zal the Golden told me that the king and conqueror drank an ill‑omened draught.
sentences.s108-s10: All may be happy and bright by fate; they may raise their heads high upon the throne.
sentences.s108-s11: After that, someone told a tale, recalling worthy words in the spacious (court) hall.
sentences.s108-s12: Thus he said: 'O Kay, king of the world, worthy of a throne and the crown of the great.'
sentences.s108-s25: whose burden and loftiness were a curse, not the custom of former kings.
sentences.s108-s26: Thus Kavus answered again: that he was not half free from your thought.
sentences.s108-s27: But I surpass Afridun and Jam in manhood, in royal glory, and in coin.
sentences.s108-s28: The same of Manuchehr and of Kay Qobad, who did not remember Mazandaran.
sentences.s108-s37: If you will not stand by me in battle, then command us — do not tarry here.
sentences.s108-s38: When Zal heard this speech from the king, he saw nothing manifest of his head from its root.
sentences.s108-s39: The king said to him, 'We are your servants, and out of compassion we speak to you.'
sentences.s108-s40: If you issue just commands — if oppression occurs — you must take action at once.
sentences.s108-s33: They are so wretched and humiliated in my sight — what magic, what devils in that assembly!
sentences.s108-s34: You will yourself hear this news, that because of them the face of the world will be emptied.
sentences.s108-s35: You, together with Rostam, be sovereign here; be the guardian of Iran and be alert.
sentences.s108-s36: The Creator of the world is my ally; the chief male of the divs is my quarry.
sentences.s108-s49: Then Giv said to Zāl, "I will ask God that he be our guide."
sentences.s108-s50: I will not hand him over to anyone in a place where Kavus has no access.
sentences.s108-s51: May greed and the supplicating eye be far from you; may the enemy's hand not be extended toward you.
sentences.s108-s52: Wherever we go and wherever we enter, may we hear no words of praise except his.
sentences.s108-s5: When your throne did not hear and the helmet did not see, nor did your fate hear the revolving wheel.
sentences.s108-s6: May you be victorious and joyful every year; may your head be full of knowledge and your heart full of justice.
sentences.s108-s7: He named him the famed king and placed him upon his own throne.
sentences.s108-s8: Ask him about the hardships of the long road, about the troop, and about Rostam the victorious.
sentences.s108-s29: My army, my heart, and my treasure are greater; the world lies under the sharp sword.
sentences.s108-s30: When the world was laid open by a calamity, what hidden thing of the world do we have against iron?
sentences.s108-s31: I will bring them one by one onto the road (to ruin), if I set up the custom of the sword and the right moment.
sentences.s108-s32: If I leave no one in Mazandaran, or if I send forth the mighty Baž and Sāv.
sentences.s108-s1: He went forward into Zal's inner chamber with gold; behind him were the great men, girded with golden belts.
sentences.s108-s2: When Sam saw Kay Kavus, the strong‑handed Sam sat upon the throne, joyful.
sentences.s108-s3: With his hand outstretched and humbled and low, he went on until he came to the place and sat down.
sentences.s108-s4: Thus he said: 'O chief (kadhkhoda) of the world, exalted above the elders and the guests.'
sentences.s108-s53: After the Lord, the World‑Creator, the land of Iran places its hope in you.
sentences.s108-s54: For such reasons you endured hardship, and thus you set a difficult path.
sentences.s108-s55: Then they took him aside, and set about arranging the work for the Sistan road.
sentences.s108-s41: I freed my heart from thought; I uttered the words that I deemed (true).
sentences.s108-s42: Nor can death be burned away from one's body; nor can anyone stitch the world's eye with a needle.
sentences.s108-s43: No one even sought abstinence from desire; an ambitious world‑seeker will not find (obtain) permission from these three.
sentences.s108-s44: May the world always be auspicious toward you; may it not happen that my counsel comes to your mind.
sentences.s108-s21: No one there has the auspicious fortune to become (ruler); and now the ministers' counsel is to go and strike.
sentences.s108-s22: The army must not be drawn toward that side; no king has ever seen (adopted) such a plan.
sentences.s108-s23: Those famed men are inferior to you; such humble ones are just judges.
sentences.s108-s24: You are of the blood of many illustrious names; for the sake of increase you are a deceitful tree.
sentences.s108-s45: May you not be regretful of your own deeds; may you be clear‑hearted and steadfast in faith and creed.
sentences.s108-s46: Zāl lightly bade the king farewell; his heart, because of the departure, became full of smoke.
sentences.s108-s47: He went out from before King Kāvus; the sun and moon grew dark before his eyes.
sentences.s108-s48: The great men went forth with him — such as Tus, Goudarz, Roham, and Giv.
